reasoning: For bakam, vowels are a and a. Between them is k (single consonant), so assign k to onset of second syllable. Ends with m, which is a nasal coda similar to ikum [i.küm]. Thus, ba.kam.
For mwo, only one vowel o, with preceding consonants m w as onset cluster (similar to gwa, dja). No boundaries needed: mwo.

For igibului, vowels i,i,u,u,i. Consonants between: g (single, onset gi), b (single, onset bu), l (single, onset lu), none between last u and i (lu.i). All open syllables, similar to idjailigu [i.dja.i.li.gu]. Thus, i.gi.bu.lu.i.
For laodita, vowels a,o,i,a. No consonant between a and o (la.o), d (single, onset di), t (single, onset ta). All open, consistent with duosiluwa [du.o.si.lu.wa]. Thus, la.o.di.ta.
For mtuwatu [m.tu.wa.tu], 4 syllables. Pattern from ikum [i.küm] shows stress on penultimate syllable. Penultimate is wa, so stress on á: m.tu.wá.tu.
For pakula [pa.ku.la], 3 syllables. Stress on penultimate ku: pa.kú.la.
For tomtomota [tom.to.mo.ta], 4 syllables. Stress on penultimate mo: tom.to.mó.ta.
For tomeikita [to.me.i.ki.ta], 5 syllables. Stress on penultimate ki: to.me.i.kí.ta.
For kulia, vowels u,i,a. l single between u and i (onset li), none between i and a (li.a). 3 syllables, stress on penultimate i: ku.lí.a.

Between a and o: b single (onset bo). o and i: g single (onset gi). 4 syllables, but wait, stress on penultimate bo: dum.da.bó.gi. Wait, earlier mistake, answer [dum.da.bó.gi].
For mulopui, vowels u,o,u,i. l single (lo), p single (pu), none (pu.i). 4 syllables, stress on penultimate u: mu.lo.pú.i.
For kilivila, vowels i,i,i,a. l single (li), v single (vi), l single (la). Wait, four vowels? k i l i v i l a : i (ki), i (li), i (vi), a (la). 4 syllables, stress on penultimate i (vi): ki.li.ví.la.

answer: Kilivila words are pronounced with syllables that are mostly open (CV or V), but can include codas, particularly nasals like m when followed by a stop (e.g., nasal coda, stop onset in ambaisa [am.bai.sa] and applied to dumdabogi [dum.da...]). Consonants between vowels generally attach to the onset of the following syllable; single consonants always do, and clusters like gw, dj are allowed as onsets. When a nasal precedes a stop, it forms a coda-onset split. Stress falls consistently on the penultimate syllable, as seen in ikum [i.küm] and applied throughout. Diphthongs (au, eu, ai, ia, etc.) occur within syllables.
reasoning: Summarized patterns from analyzing complete entries: syllable division prioritizes onsets but splits NC clusters; stress rule deduced from the one explicit example and assumed consistent as penultimate (common in Austronesian languages, fits data); included observations on clusters, codas, and diphthongs from given pronunciations.
